title: Reforming, Not Reformed
dek: This isn't a redemption arc. It's a work in progress, and I'm done pretending otherwise.
content: Everyone wants the after-photo. The clean apartment, the green juice, the caption about 'healing.' Nobody wants the part where you're still figuring it out at 1am with your phone face-down on the table because you don't trust yourself with it yet.

I'm Rama Ben Khalifa, and I want to be very clear about something before you read anything else on this site: I have not arrived anywhere. I am reforming, present continuous, not reformed, past tense. Those are different words for a reason.
> A redemption arc has an ending. Mine doesn't come with one, and I stopped waiting for the credits to roll.

For a long time the story other people told about me had a shape: party girl, cautionary tale, mess. It was easier for them than the truth, which is that I am a Gemini sun who can hold six versions of myself at once, a Scorpio moon who feels everything like a verdict, and a person who has spent years testing exactly how far 'off the rails' could go before something broke for good.
Something did break, more than once. I'm not going to smooth that over here just to make myself easier to root for.

What I can tell you is that reforming, for me, doesn't look like willpower. It looks like writing this down in public, where I can't quietly take it back tomorrow. It looks like naming the pattern instead of just living inside it. This magazine is that — not a highlight reel, not a warning label. Just the log, story by story, of a person trying to become someone she can trust.

title: Rock Bottom Had Wifi
dek: On the night everything caught up with me, and why I'm grateful it did.
content: Rock bottom, for me, did not look cinematic. There was no rain, no mascara running down my face in slow motion. There was a hallway floor, a phone at 4% battery, and the specific, humiliating clarity of realizing I could not remember the last two hours of my own life.

I want to write around this story. I've written around it for a long time, told the version with better lighting to people at dinner. Here's the plainer one: I had been telling myself 'one more night' for months, and that night the bill came due in a way I couldn't negotiate with.
> The scariest part wasn't the blackout. It was how unbothered I was by it the next morning, until I wasn't.

There's a strange grief in outgrowing a version of yourself you also loved, a little. That girl was fun. People wanted her at the party. She got invited everywhere and belonged nowhere, including inside her own head.

I'm not telling this story for sympathy and I'm not telling it as a morality tale either. I'm telling it because pretending it didn't happen is exactly the kind of quiet dishonesty that let it happen fifty times before. Reforming a party girl starts with stopping the edit.

title: The Court Date
dek: What it's like to sit in a room and answer for the version of you that stopped listening.
content: There's a particular kind of quiet in a room where a stranger in a robe is about to say your full name out loud. Rama Ben Khalifa. Not the nickname my friends use, not the handle on my old party photos — the whole legal weight of it.

I'm not going to use this space to relitigate the specifics of what got me there. That's not the point of the story, and honestly it's not anyone's business but mine and the people directly involved. What I will say is that consequences had been circling for a while, and I had gotten very good at outrunning them, until the day I couldn't.
> I used to think getting away with something meant I'd won. That day I learned the difference between getting away with it and getting away from it.

Sitting there, I kept doing this thing where I tried to perform remorse correctly, like there was a rubric. Eventually I stopped performing and just felt it, which was worse and also, weirdly, the first honest thing I'd done in months.

I left that building different than I walked in. Not fixed — I want to resist that word every time it tries to sneak into this site — but accountable in a way that finally stuck to my skin instead of sliding off. Some lessons you can only learn from someone with the actual authority to make you sit still long enough to hear them.

title: Sober Tuesdays (And Why They Never Stuck)
dek: The on-again, off-again truth about trying to quit a version of yourself.
content: I have quit partying more times than I can count, which is another way of saying I have never actually quit partying. Every 'this is it' lasted exactly as long as it took for the next invitation to feel more interesting than my own resolve.

The pattern always started the same way: a hard reset on a Monday, a spreadsheet-brained version of me who journaled and drank green tea and felt, briefly, like a completely different woman. By Thursday that woman was gone and I was back to being the one everyone actually expected to show up.
> I didn't have an off-and-on relationship with partying. I had an off-and-on relationship with believing I deserved better than the chaos.

Type B on the surface, borderline underneath — I run hot and I run out. Discipline that isn't attached to something I actually feel doesn't survive contact with a Friday night. I know that about myself now, which is different from managing it, but it's not nothing.

This piece isn't a victory lap about finally sticking the landing. I'm publishing it because the honest, unglamorous middle — the part where you fail the same promise on a loop — is the part nobody puts in the caption, and I think it's the part that actually matters.

title: Scorpio Moon, Broken Rules
dek: What my chart says about why I always went too far — and why I don't blame the stars anymore.
content: Gemini sun, Scorpio moon, Scorpio rising. I've used this chart as an explanation, an excuse, and eventually just a description, in roughly that order.

The Gemini sun wants novelty, wants six conversations at once, gets bored of a room before it's finished being interesting. The Scorpio moon and rising want intensity or nothing — no polite middle setting, no 'a little bit' of a feeling. Put those in one body and you get someone who doesn't just go to the party, she needs the party to mean something, to be the most anything that has ever happened.
> For years I said 'it's a Scorpio moon thing' the way other people say 'it's just how I'm wired,' as if that ended the conversation instead of starting it.

Here's what I've come around to: the chart explains the shape of the hunger, not the choices I made to feed it. A Scorpio moon doesn't write a check with your name on it. A Scorpio moon doesn't sit in a courtroom for you either.

I still love my chart. I still think it's a genuinely useful map of why I feel like the volume knob is missing on my own emotions. But I've stopped handing it the steering wheel. These days it's context, not a defense.

title: What My Friends Won't Say To My Face
dek: The group chat I wasn't in, the interventions that weren't, and the friendships that didn't survive the old me.
content: I found out about the group chat by accident, the way you find out about most things people are protecting you from. It wasn't cruel. It was worse than cruel — it was careful, the kind of careful people get when they've quietly downgraded you from 'friend having a rough patch' to 'friend we're managing.'

Nobody staged an intervention. Nobody sat me down. Instead, invitations got quieter. Check-ins got shorter. People started saying 'let's do something low-key' in a tone that meant something specific about me they weren't going to say out loud.
> The scariest thing isn't being called out. It's realizing people cared enough to talk about you and not enough, anymore, to talk to you.

Some of those friendships I've since repaired, slowly, by being a person worth trusting again instead of asking to be trusted on credit. Some of them are just gone, and I've had to make peace with the fact that I don't get to relitigate that with people who already made their decision while I wasn't looking.
I'm writing this one for anyone who's ever found their own 'concerned' group chat. It doesn't feel like love in the moment. Sometimes it is anyway.
